We have, you know, kind of an interesting role here that is that does require the ability to, like, at a glance, understand the quality level of content to know what the building blocks of it are and kind of instinctually grasp, hey, this seems like it's getting to where it needs to be for a finished product.

But the other side of the role is very much about articulating that and helping the automated workflows that we've created to get better and to be able to manipulate the inputs of them to get the desired outputs and to understand the way that inputs to workflows or pipelines of workflows, because we build workflows and then we connect them together to create complex pipelines, what the inputs are that are going in, which ones might affect the issues you're seeing that you are able to articulate in the output, and then, of course, adjusting those inputs, therefore.
The reason I lay that out is because I think there are amazing people out there in both of those worlds, but it's much harder to find people who have a solid overlap between them.
That's what we're trying to do because the ability to write well is, you would think, would be really core to the content creation world that we're in.
And it is to some degree, mean, a good writer is sort of table sticks, but the ability to then articulate how writing is done is not as common.
Typically in the content world, we would call them editors, but the word editor has been diluted massively over the past few decades, right?

And so the editor in differentiation from the writer is able to like pull apart component aspects, understand where the structure or architecture of it might be going astray or where it needs to be bolstered, and then be able to articulate that to a writer.
In our case, the writer is

So that's why we're looking more for that persona than somebody who can just write really well instinctually, because then that lives here, and maybe they're unable to articulate it, and therefore the workflows don't get better, or we're unable to kind of fix problems for them because they're like, I don't know, it's bad, you know, why?
Well, I don't know, it's like short.

You know, one of the core things to understand about prompting is that generally speaking, reinforcement of prompts and what they call chain of thought prompting is super important for prompts because you need to, or LLMs, because you need to tell them, here are my expectations for you.
Here's the role that you're playing.
And here's the task that I have for you.
And here's the format or architecture in which I would like you to perform that.
If it doesn't have those things, that prompt can lead it, it can produce all kinds of wacky results, and then it's very difficult to like chew back to where you were.
You normally have to tell her, cancel, but I'm going to go start a new chat, right?
But yeah, having explicit feedback and then reinforcing that feedback is really important in saying like, that's perfect, that's great, that's exactly what I want.
